q: In the spoken sentence, 'I didn't say *he* stole the money,' stressing the word 'he' implies that:
opts: I don't think any money was stolen.
Someone else may have stolen the money.
I said he stole something else.
I am certain he is innocent.
exp: When you stress 'he' in that sentence, my dear, it means you might be suggesting that someone *else* is the one who stole the money, not necessarily him.

q: Which word has the same vowel sound as <u>meet</u>?
exp: The vowel sound in 'meet' is a long 'ee' sound. 'Beat' shares this exact sound, while 'met' and 'mate' have different vowel sounds.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'b' when spoken?
exp: In 'comb', the 'b' is silent, you see. Words like this often come from older English spellings, and the pronunciation has changed over time.

q: When practicing active listening, what is the primary purpose of paraphrasing the speaker's words?
opts: To show you have a better vocabulary
To confirm your understanding of the message
To change the subject of discussion
exp: Repeating what the speaker said in your own words, my dear, is the best way to make sure you both understand the same thing. It ensures no wires get crossed.

q: Which of the following words rhymes with <u>wear</u>?
exp: You see, 'stare' and 'wear' have the same ending sound, like 'air'. The other options, while close, don't quite rhyme perfectly with 'wear'.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'PHOtograph'?
exp: For 'PHOtograph', the emphasis falls clearly on the first syllable, 'PHO'. This is the standard pronunciation for this word.

q: Which word is a homophone of the spoken word 'blew'?
exp: Homophones sound the same but have different spellings and meanings. 'Blew' (past tense of blow) and 'blue' (the colour) sound identical, making them homophones.

q: A falling intonation in spoken English is typically used at the end of:
exp: When you make a statement, my dear, your voice usually goes down at the end. This falling tone signals that the sentence is complete.

q: A speaker says: 'Well, I suppose we could try that, but...' What does the tone and the word 'but' most likely signal?
exp: The phrase 'could try that, but...' shows some doubt or unwillingness, doesn't it? It signals that the speaker is hesitant about the idea.

q: If a speaker says, 'I wanted *coffee*, not tea,' the stress on 'coffee' is meant to:
exp: Saying 'I wanted *coffee*, not tea' is a way to directly correct a mistake someone made. You are clarifying what you actually wanted.

q: Which of the following words has a silent 'k'?
exp: In the word 'knee', the 'k' comes before the 'n', and it's silent. We don't pronounce it at all when we say the word.

q: Which of the following words has a different vowel sound from the others?
exp: The vowel sound in 'food' is a long 'oo' sound, like in 'moon'. 'Blood', 'flood', and 'mud' all share a different short 'u' sound.

q: In a conversation, nodding your head and saying 'uh-huh' is an example of:
exp: Nodding and saying 'uh-huh' are ways to show you're listening and paying attention without interrupting. It encourages the speaker to continue.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'computer'?
exp: In 'computer', the main stress is on the last part, the 'ter'. This gives the word its natural rhythm when spoken.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>rain</u>?
exp: The words 'rain' and 'vein' have the same ending sound, like 'ain'. They rhyme perfectly, my dear.

q: If a speaker says, 'Could you run that by me one more time?', they are asking you to:
exp: When someone says 'run that by me', they simply want you to repeat what you said, my dear. It's a polite way to ask for clarification.

q: A rising intonation is most commonly heard at the end of which spoken sentence?
exp: A rising intonation often signals a question, especially a yes/no question like this one. You're asking for confirmation, so the voice goes up at the end.

q: Which word has a silent 'w' when spoken?
exp: In English, some words have letters that are there but not pronounced. 'Write' is one of those words; the 'w' is silent.

q: In the spoken sentence, 'She didn't write *that* essay,' the stress on 'that' implies:
opts: She didn't write any essay at all.
She wrote the essay very quickly.
exp: Stressing 'that' here means she wrote a different essay, not the one being discussed. It's about changing the focus to a specific item.

q: Which of the following words has the same consonant sound as the 'ch' in 'chemist'?
exp: The sound in 'character' is a 'k' sound, just like the 'ch' in 'chemist'. It's a hard 'c' sound, you see.

q: Which of the following options contains a silent 't'?
exp: In 'castle', the 't' is silent. Many English words have these silent letters, and 'castle' is a common example.

q: In active listening, maintaining eye contact and facing the speaker directly are examples of:
exp: Facing the speaker and making eye contact shows you are paying attention without using words. These are non-verbal signals of good listening.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'tomorrow'?
exp: The stress in 'tomorrow' falls on the second syllable, 'mor'. That's where you put the emphasis when you say it.

q: Which word rhymes with the spoken word 'tough'?
exp: Both 'tough' and 'rough' end with the same 'uff' sound. They are perfect rhymes, my dear.

q: A speaker says: 'I'm swamped with work and feel completely overwhelmed.' An active listener would paraphrase this by saying:
opts: 'It sounds like you have too much on your plate right now.'
exp: Paraphrasing shows you understand. Saying 'It sounds like you have too much on your plate' restates the speaker's feeling of being overwhelmed in different words.

q: Which of the following questions is an 'open question' designed to encourage a speaker to share more details?
opts: 'What was your favorite part of the trip?'
exp: Open questions like 'What was your favorite part...?' encourage detailed answers and more conversation. They can't be answered with a simple yes or no.

q: Which word contains a silent 'h' when spoken?
exp: The 'h' in 'honest' is silent. We pronounce it as if it starts with a vowel sound.

q: If a speaker says, 'I *never* want to see that movie again,' the stressed word 'never' indicates:
opts: A request for a movie recommendation
exp: Stressing 'never' in this context shows a very strong feeling, in this case, strong dislike for the movie. It emphasizes the point.

q: Which word has the same vowel sound as the one in <u>keys</u>?
exp: The word 'keys' has a long 'ee' sound, and 'cheese' shares that exact same vowel sound.

q: Which word has a silent 'g' when spoken?
exp: 'Sign' is a word where the 'g' is not pronounced. The sound comes from the 'n' that follows it.

q: To show you are actively listening, which phrase is best to use when clarifying information?
exp: That's right, my dear! Using 'So, what you are saying is...' shows you're checking your understanding and actively engaged in the conversation. It's a good way to confirm you've heard correctly.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'beautiful'?
exp: Yes, the emphasis is on the first syllable, 'beau', in 'beautiful'. Practicing pronunciation helps a lot, you see.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>height</u>?
exp: Exactly! The word 'bite' has the same ending sound as 'height'. Words don't always look like they sound, and this is a good example.

q: Which of the following is a homophone of 'know'?
exp: Well done! 'No' sounds exactly like 'know', which is what makes them homophones. They are spelled differently but sound the same.

q: A rising intonation is generally used when speaking:
opts: A list item that is final
exp: Correct! A rising intonation at the end of a sentence usually signals a question that can be answered with 'yes' or 'no'. It makes the listener expect a response.

q: Which word has a silent 'l' when spoken?
exp: That's it! In 'talk', the 'l' is silent, so we don't pronounce it. It's just one of those English spelling quirks we have to learn.

q: If a speaker says, 'I didn't steal *your* laptop,' the stress on 'your' implies:
opts: I stole a tablet, not a laptop.
exp: Precisely. Stressing 'your' changes the whole meaning to suggest another person's laptop was taken. It highlights that specific word.

q: Which of the following words has the same vowel sound as the one in <u>sun</u>?
exp: You've got it! 'Done' has that same 'uh' sound as in 'sun'. It's important to listen closely to vowel sounds in English.

q: Which word has a silent 'p' when spoken?
exp: Excellent! The 'p' in 'receipt' is silent. Words like this can be tricky, but recognizing the silent letters helps with pronunciation.

q: What is the primary barrier to effective listening known as 'information overload'?
opts: Receiving too much data at once to process
exp: That's a good grasp of it. Information overload means your brain simply can't process all the incoming data effectively at that moment. It's too much!

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'REcord' (when used as a noun)?
exp: Indeed. For the noun 'REcord', the stress falls on the first syllable, 'RE'. This is common in English where nouns and verbs can have different stress patterns.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>cough</u>?
exp: Correct! The word 'off' rhymes perfectly with 'cough'. Some English words have tricky pronunciations, and this is one of them.

q: If a speaker says, 'I'd love to go, *but* I have an exam,' what does the speaker's tone and stress on 'but' show?
opts: Hesitation or regret about not going
exp: Yes, that's it. The word 'but' here is stressed to show a contrast or hesitation, indicating the speaker wishes they could go but has a conflict. It shows regret.

q: Which of the following words has the same consonant sound as the 'g' in 'germ'?
exp: You are absolutely correct! The 'g' in 'giant' makes a 'j' sound, just like in 'germ'. This is common with 'g' followed by 'i' or 'e'.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'd'?
exp: That's right! The 'd' in 'Wednesday' is typically silent when we say it. It's one of those words where the spelling doesn't quite match the spoken sound.

q: An active listener who asks 'What do you mean by that?' is using which listening sub-skill?
exp: When you ask 'What do you mean by that?', my dear, you are seeking more information to understand clearly. This is the essence of clarifying your understanding.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'decision'?
exp: The last syllable, '-sion', receives the emphasis when you say 'de-ci-SION'. This is how we naturally stress this word in English.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>shoe</u>?
exp: The words 'shoe' and 'blue' share the same long 'oo' sound, making them rhyme perfectly. Isn't that lovely?

q: If a speaker says, 'We need to talk about the *budget*,' the stress on 'budget' suggests:
opts: The speaker is happy about the money.
The budget is the specific issue that needs attention.
The speaker wants to cancel the talk.
exp: Stressing 'budget' shows that this particular topic is what the speaker wants to focus on. It's the main point of the discussion.

q: Which word is a homophone of 'deer'?
exp: 'Deer' and 'dear' sound exactly the same when spoken, even though they are spelled differently. That's what a homophone is.

q: Which of the following words has a silent 'u' when spoken?
exp: In 'guard', the 'u' doesn't create its own sound, you see. It modifies the 'g' sound, and we don't pronounce it separately.

q: When listening to a presentation, what is 'critical listening'?
opts: Listening only to find errors in pronunciation
Listening to analyze and evaluate the message
exp: Critical listening means you are actively thinking about what is being said, judging its worth and truthfulness. You're not just hearing it passively.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'engineer'?
exp: The last part of the word, '-neer', is where the stress falls in 'en-gin-EER'. It's very common for longer words to have stress on the final or penultimate syllable.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>bear</u>?
exp: The words 'bear' and 'pear' rhyme because they have the same ending sound. They both end with the 'air' sound.

q: If a speaker says, 'I cannot *possibly* agree to those terms,' the stress on 'possibly' emphasizes:
exp: Putting an extra emphasis on 'possibly' when saying 'I cannot *possibly* agree' makes it clear that the refusal is complete and strong. There is no room for negotiation.

q: Which of the following words has the same vowel sound as the one in <u>pain</u>?
exp: The vowel sound in 'pain' is the same long 'ay' sound you hear in 'rein'. They are spelled differently, but sound alike.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'n'?
exp: In 'solemn', the 'n' is not pronounced at all. It's like it's there, but not part of the spoken word.

q: In a formal lecture, listening for main ideas rather than every word is called:
exp: Global listening, or gist listening, is when you focus on the main points and overall message of a talk, not every single word. It helps grasp the big picture.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'important'?
exp: The stress in 'im-POR-tant' falls on the second syllable. This is a key feature of how we pronounce this common word.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>steak</u>?
exp: 'Steak' and 'break' rhyme beautifully because they share the same long 'ake' sound at the end. Simple as that!

q: If a speaker says, 'I thought *you* were going to call him,' the stress on 'you' implies:
opts: I didn't think anyone should call him.
I expected you to do the calling, not someone else.
I was going to call him myself.
I don't know who he is.
exp: When you stress 'you' in that sentence, my dear, it simply means that your expectation was for *that particular person* to make the call, not just anyone.

q: Which word is a homophone of the spoken word 'flour'?
exp: Exactly! 'Flour' and 'flower' sound identical when spoken, even though they have different meanings and spellings. That's what a homophone is.

q: Which of the following words has a silent 'c' when spoken?
exp: Ah yes, 'muscle' has a silent 'c' before the 'l'. You don't pronounce it when you say the word, nice one!

q: To show empathy while listening to a friend, what is the best verbal response?
opts: 'I understand how difficult this must be for you.'
'That is nothing compared to my day.'
'Why does this always happen to you?'
exp: This response shows you truly hear your friend and acknowledge their feelings. It's about validating their experience, you see.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'academic'?
exp: For 'academic,' the stress falls on the third syllable: a-ca-DEM-ic. That's where the emphasis naturally lies when you say it.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>breathe</u>?
exp: Lovely! Both 'breathe' and 'seethe' have that long 'ee' sound at the end, making them rhyme perfectly.

q: If a speaker says, 'It was *her* idea, not mine,' the stress on 'her' is meant to:
exp: By stressing 'her,' the speaker is clearly pointing out that the idea originated from her, not from the speaker themselves. It shifts the ownership.

q: Which of the following words has the same consonant sound as the 's' in 'treasure'?
exp: The 's' in 'treasure' makes a 'zh' sound. The 's' in 'sight' also makes that same 'zh' sound. It's a common sound pattern.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 't'?
exp: 'Listen' has a silent 't'. We just don't pronounce it when we say the word, even though it's written there.

q: In oral communication, 'intonation' refers to:
opts: The rise and fall in the pitch of the voice
The speed at which words are spoken
exp: Intonation is all about the musicality of your voice, you see. It’s how the pitch goes up and down to convey meaning.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'biology'?
exp: For 'biology,' the stress is on the second syllable: bi-OL-o-gy. That's the part that sounds strongest when you say it.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>mind</u>?
exp: Indeed! 'Mind' and 'find' share the same ending sound, making them rhyme beautifully.

q: If a speaker says, 'We must act *now*,' the stress on 'now' emphasizes:
exp: Stressing 'now' really drives home the point that immediate action is needed. It shows there's no time to waste!

q: Which word is a homophone of 'knight'?
exp: Yes, 'knight' and 'night' sound exactly the same, though they mean completely different things. They are perfect homophones.

q: Which of the following words has a silent 'g' when spoken?
exp: The 'g' in 'gnat' is completely silent! You don't make any sound for it when you pronounce the word.

q: When listening to a speech, identifying the speaker's tone helps the listener understand:
opts: The spelling of the words used
The speaker's attitude or emotion toward the topic
The exact length of the speech
exp: My dear, the speaker's tone is like their voice color! It shows how they feel about the subject, whether happy, sad, or serious. That's why option B is correct.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'university'?
exp: You see, in 'university', the stress falls on the third syllable: 'ver-SI-ty'. This is where you give the word a little more emphasis when you say it.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>doubt</u>?
exp: When you say 'doubt', the ending sound is like 'out'. 'Shout' also ends with that same 'out' sound, making them rhyme perfectly.

q: If a speaker says, 'I am *absolutely* certain of the results,' the stress on 'absolutely' shows:
exp: Putting extra force on 'absolutely' here shows the speaker is extremely sure and has no doubts at all. It's all about intensity, my friend.

q: Which of the following words has the same vowel sound as the one in <u>cat</u>?
exp: The vowel sound in 'cat' is short and sharp. 'Plaid' has exactly that same sound, unlike 'car' or 'cot'.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'w'?
exp: Simple! In 'wrong', the 'w' is just there for show; it's not pronounced at all. That's why it's silent.

q: When listening for 'signpost words' (like 'firstly', 'however', 'in conclusion'), the listener is:
opts: Following the structure and transition of the speech
exp: Signpost words are like road signs in a speech, my dear. They guide you through the speaker's thoughts and show how ideas connect.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'education'?
exp: For 'education', the emphasis lands on the third syllable: 'ca-TION'. You hear that stronger beat there.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>weight</u>?
exp: Say 'weight' and 'plate' aloud. You'll notice they end with the same vowel and consonant sound, making them rhyme beautifully.

q: If a speaker says, 'He is *very* intelligent,' the stress on 'very' acts as:
exp: Stressing 'very' here makes the word 'intelligent' sound much stronger. It's an intensifier, showing just how intelligent the person is.

q: Which word is a homophone of 'bare'?
exp: A homophone sounds the same but has a different meaning and spelling. 'Bare' and 'bear' sound alike, but 'bear' can be an animal, you see.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'h'?
exp: In 'ghost', the 'h' is silent. It doesn't affect the sound of the word at all when you pronounce it.

q: When listening to instructions, what is the best strategy to ensure you do not miss a step?
opts: Listen to only the first and last steps
Take notes of key verbs and sequence words
Try to memorize every single word spoken
Interrupt the speaker after every word
exp: Taking notes helps you track the steps and remember them better. It's much more effective than trying to keep everything in your head!

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'celebration'?
exp: When you say 'celebration', the strongest part is the third syllable: 'BRA'. You feel the emphasis there.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>plough</u>?
exp: For 'plough', the sound is like 'ow' as in 'cow'. 'Rough', 'dough', and 'cough' have different ending sounds, my dear.

q: If a speaker says, 'I did *not* agree to this,' the stress on 'not' emphasizes:
exp: Excellent! Stressing 'not' shows a strong refusal or denial, my dear. It means the speaker absolutely did not agree to whatever was proposed.

q: Which of the following words has the same consonant sound as the 's' in 'sugar'?
exp: Well done! The consonant sound in 'sugar' is /ʃ/, like the 'sh' sound you hear in 'shoe'. The other words have different sounds.

q: Which of the following words contains a silent 'l'?
exp: That's right! In 'should', the 'l' is silent. We don't pronounce it at all when we say the word. The other options have a pronounced 'l'.

q: In a spoken list like 'apples, oranges, and bananas,' intonation typically:
opts: Rises on the first two and falls on the last
Falls on the first two and rises on the last
exp: Exactly! Intonation typically rises on list items to show there's more to come, and then falls on the very last item to signal the end of the list.

q: Which syllable is stressed in the spoken word 'politics'?
exp: Correct! In 'politics', the main stress falls on the first syllable: PO-li-tics. This is a common stress pattern for many three-syllable words.

q: Which word rhymes with <u>lose</u>?
exp: Very good! 'Choose' rhymes with 'lose' because they share the same ending vowel and consonant sound. Listen to them: lose, choose.

q: If a speaker says, 'We must find a *better* way,' the stress on 'better' implies:
opts: The current way is inadequate and needs improvement.
We should stop looking for ways.
exp: Precisely! Stressing 'better' clearly tells us the current way is not good enough and we need to find something superior. It highlights dissatisfaction.

q: Which word is a homophone of 'son'?
exp: Yes, that's absolutely correct! 'Son' and 'sun' sound exactly the same when spoken, even though they are spelled differently and have different meanings.

q: Which of the following words has a silent 'b' when spoken?
exp: You got it! In the word 'debt', the 'b' is silent. We don't make that sound when we speak the word. It's a common silent letter.

q: Active listening is highly important in communication because it:
opts: Allows you to think of your reply while the other person is speaking
Minimizes misunderstandings and shows respect to the speaker
Forces the speaker to agree with your opinion
Saves time by cutting the speaker off early
exp: That's a great point! Active listening means paying full attention, which helps prevent confusion and shows you value what the speaker is saying.
